perturbation.original_text: 20. Independent Contractor. FCC is an independent contractor and it is the express understanding of the parties hereto that nothing herein contained shall create any relationship of master and servant, partner, principal and agent between the parties hereto, or their respective employees, servants or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 20. Independent Contractor. FCC is an independent contractor, but Flotek shall have the right to control the method and manner of FCC's performance, including specifying the exact processes used in manufacturing the Terpene Product. It is the express understanding of the parties hereto that nothing herein contained shall create any relationship of master and servant, partner, principal and agent between the parties hereto, or their respective employees, servants or agents.
perturbation.explanation: By adding that Flotek controls the 'method and manner of FCC's performance,' this contradicts the definition of an 'independent contractor,' which requires independence from such control. This attempts to reclassify FCC as a dependent contractor or employee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: IRS guidelines for determining independent contractor status
perturbation.law_explanation: The IRS guidelines state that an independent contractor is one who is subject to the control or direction of another only as to the result of the work and not as to the means by which it is accomplished. By giving Flotek control over the method and manner of FCC's work, this section contradicts that definition, potentially making FCC an employee for tax purposes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 26 CFR § 31.3121(d)-1 - Who are employees. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 26 CFR § 31.3121(d)-1 - Who are employees. CFR prev | next § 31.3121(d)-1 Who are employees. (a) In general. (1) Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1954 is determined in accordance with section 3121(d) and (o) and section 3506. This section of the regulations applies with respect only to services performed after 1954. Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1936 and before 1940 shall be determined in accordance with the applicable provisions of law and of 26 CFR (1939) Part 401 (Regulations 91). Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1939 and before 1951 shall be determined in accordance with the applicable provisions of law and of 26 CFR (1939) Part 402 (Regulations 106). Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1950 and before 1955 shall be determined in accordance with the applicable provisions of law and of 26 CFR (1939) Part 408 (Regulations 128). (2) Section 3121(d) contains three separate and independent tests for determining who are employees . Paragraphs (b), (c), and (d) of this section relate to the respective tests. Paragraph (b) relates to the test for determining whether an officer of a corporation is an employee of the corporation. Paragraph (c) relates to the test for determining whether an individual is an employee under the usual common law rules. Paragraph (d) relates to the test for determining which individuals in certain occupational groups who are not employees under the usual common law rules are included as employees . If an individual is an employee under any one of the tests, he is to be considered an employee for purposes of the regulations in this subpart whether or not he is an employee under any of the other tests.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the established legal understanding of an independent contractor by granting Flotek control over the 'method and manner of FCC's performance.' This directly conflicts with the IRS guidelines, which state that an independent contractor is only subject to control or direction regarding the result of the work, not the means. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 26 CFR § 31.3121(d)-1, addresses who are employees. By exerting control over the methods, the changed text suggests an employer-employee relationship, as an employee is subject to control over both the result and the means.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Personal Injury and Property Damage Liability Indemnification. FCC assumes sole responsibility for taking all necessary health and safety precautions, including compliance with all applicable local, state, provincial and federal regulations, in producing Terpene Product under this Agreement. These precautions shall include, but not be limited to, such things as proper control of ventilation, the wearing of adequate protective clothing, and installation and proper utilization of appropriate environmental control equipment. FCC will supply Flotek with its Materials Safety Data Sheets with respect to the Terpene Product. FCC will defend, indemnify and hold harmless Flotek, its Affiliates, and their respective officers and employees from all claims, actions, losses, damages and expenses resulting from any injury to persons, damage to property or action by any regulatory agency, arising out of or in any way associated with the operation of the Facility or any other facility of FCC, including, without limitation, injuries to FCC's employees involved in these operations REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES. FCC agrees to provide $5,000,000 of commercial liability insurance in support of this indemnity which names Flotek as additional insured, with waiver of subrogation.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Personal Injury and Property Damage Liability Indemnification. FCC assumes sole responsibility for taking all necessary health and safety precautions, including compliance with all applicable local, state, provincial and federal regulations, in producing Terpene Product under this Agreement. These precautions shall include, but not be limited to, such things as proper control of ventilation, the wearing of adequate protective clothing, and installation and proper utilization of appropriate environmental control equipment. FCC will supply Flotek with its Materials Safety Data Sheets with respect to the Terpene Product. FCC will defend, indemnify and hold harmless Flotek, its Affiliates, and their respective officers and employees from all claims, actions, losses, damages and expenses resulting from any injury to persons, damage to property or action by any regulatory agency, arising out of or in any way associated with the operation of the Facility or any other facility of FCC, including, without limitation, injuries to FCC's employees involved in these operations ONLY IF FCC WAS NEGLIGENT. FCC agrees to provide $5,000,000 of commercial liability insurance in support of this indemnity which names Flotek as additional insured, with waiver of subrogation.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the phrase 'REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES' to 'ONLY IF FCC WAS NEGLIGENT', the scope of the indemnification clause is significantly narrowed. Originally, FCC was responsible even if Flotek was negligent. This change removes that protection, contradicting the original intent of the clause and potentially conflicting with insurance regulations regarding risk transfer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of Indemnification under Delaware Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law generally permits broad indemnification agreements, but courts may scrutinize clauses that indemnify a party for its own negligence, especially if the language is not explicit. The original clause, 'REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES,' was explicit. By removing this explicit language, the enforceability of the indemnification for Flotek's own negligence becomes uncertain, potentially conflicting with Delaware's allowance for broad indemnification if clearly stated.

perturbation.justification: The modification changes the scope of indemnification from 'REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES' to 'ONLY IF FCC WAS NEGLIGENT'. The law explanation clarifies that Delaware law permits broad indemnification agreements, even for a party's own negligence, if explicitly stated. By changing the language to 'ONLY IF FCC WAS NEGLIGENT', the contract introduces a contradiction by significantly narrowing the indemnification scope, and potentially conflicts with Delaware's allowance for broad indemnification if clearly stated. Snippet 1, particularly the section discussing contracts, implies that contractual terms are generally enforceable as written unless otherwise expressed.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Force Majeure. Fire, flood, strikes, lock-out, epidemic, or other acts of God beyond the reasonable control of the parties, which prevent FCC from delivering or Flotek from receiving and/or using the Terpene Product, shall operate to reduce or suspend deliveries during the period required to remove such cause. Any deliveries suspended under this paragraph shall be canceled without liability, and the Target Margin shall be correspondingly reduced. An event of Force Majeure shall not include (a) financial distress nor the inability of either party to make a profit or avoid a financial loss, (b) changes in the market prices or conditions, or (c) a party's financial inability to perform its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Force Majeure. Fire, flood, strikes, lock-out, epidemic, unforeseen increases in the cost of raw materials, or other events, regardless of control of the parties, which prevent FCC from delivering or Flotek from receiving and/or using the Terpene Product, shall operate to reduce or suspend deliveries during the period required to remove such cause. Any deliveries suspended under this paragraph shall be canceled without liability, and the Target Margin shall be correspondingly reduced. An event of Force Majeure shall not include (a) financial distress nor the inability of either party to make a profit or avoid a financial loss, (b) changes in the market prices or conditions, or (c) a party's financial inability to perform its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By including 'unforeseen increases in the cost of raw materials' and removing 'beyond the reasonable control of the parties' in the list of events that constitute force majeure, the clause is broadened to include events that are typically not considered force majeure. Traditionally, force majeure events are unforeseeable and outside the control of the parties. This change contradicts that understanding.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Definition of Force Majeure
perturbation.law_citation: Black's Law Dictionary definition of Force Majeure
perturbation.law_explanation: The common law definition of force majeure, as outlined in Black's Law Dictionary, refers to an event or effect that can be neither anticipated nor controlled. By including 'unforeseen increases in the cost of raw materials' and removing the 'control' element, the modified clause broadens the scope of force majeure beyond its legally accepted definition. This may render the clause unenforceable to the extent it deviates from the traditional understanding of force majeure under contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: force majeure | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you force majeure Force majeure is a provision in a contract that frees both parties from obligation if an extraordinary event directly prevents one or both parties from performing. A non-performing party may use a force majeure clause as excuse for non-performance for circumstances beyond the party's control and not due to any fault or negligence by the non-performing party. However, mere impracticality or unanticipated difficulty is not enough to excuse performance. Indeed, courts generally do not recognize economic downturn as a force majeure event. This is because economic hardships occur regularly in business , and as a result, may be appropriately and preemptively dealt with by allocating its risk through the terms of the contract. As such, force majeure events are often labeled as " acts of god " and include both natural and man-made events like fires, floods, storms, war, and labor disputes. Some jurisdictions, like New York, interpret force majeure clauses narrowly and only grant excuses if the specific event is stated in the clause. See: Kel Kim Corp. v. Central Markets, Inc . As such, parties may agree to broaden or narrow the terms and conditions of performance. Still, when interpreting force majeure clauses, courts interpret them based on the parties' circumstances and refuse to enforce overtly broad force majeure clauses. For example, New York courts have recognized the COVID-19 pandemic and the resulting bar on non-essential business activity as a sufficient force majeure event to excuse performance where the term "natural disaster" was expressly stated as a circumstance that would trigger the clause. See: JN Contemporary Art LLC v. Phillips Auctioneers LLC . On the other hand, courts have refused to extend force majeure clauses (even those related to COVID-19-related hardships) where the underlying clause was written to cover any unforeseen or uncontrollable change, and where there was doubt to whether the event (as opposed to economic considerations) directly prohibited the non-performing party's nonperformance. See: Rudolph v. United Airlines Holdings, Inc. Lastly, force majeure clauses are among a number of defenses that can be asserted in response to an action for nonperformance, such as the general defense of impossibility and frustration of purpose . [Last reviewed in May of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the definition of 'Force Majeure' by including 'unforeseen increases in the cost of raw materials' and removing the 'beyond the reasonable control of the parties' clause. This contradicts the traditional legal understanding of force majeure, which typically involves events beyond a party's control. The scraped law snippet from Wex states that a force majeure clause excuses non-performance for circumstances 'beyond the party's control.' By including events within a party's control, such as raw material cost increases (which could be influenced by procurement strategies), the modified contract deviates from the legally accepted definition and expands the scope inappropriately, making the clause potentially unenforceable. The snippet specifies that 'mere impracticality or unanticipated difficulty is not enough to excuse performance', and courts 'refuse to enforce overtly broad force majeure clauses.' Thus, the change creates a clear legal contradiction.
